title: Today's Markets Brief: US stock futures steady post record Wall St close; Iran strikes, PCE data in focus
summary: A look at the steady US stock futures, Iran's recent strikes, and upcoming PCE data.

body.text: I'm Leo Stone, and welcome to your markets brief. US stock futures are holding steady after Wall Street closed at a record high. The big question is: what's next? Let's dive into the headlines. It's always exciting to see these records being broken, and I'm curious to see how things unfold.

First up, US stock futures are steady. This comes after a record close on Wall Street. The market seems to be taking a breather. Meanwhile, there's been some movement with Iran. They've been making headlines with their recent strikes. This development has the potential to impact oil prices, which have been a bit of a rollercoaster lately. It's a good reminder that global events can quickly change the market mood.

The Federal Reserve's Austan Goolsbee shared some insights on energy inflation. According to him, energy inflation has been more persistent than expected. This is interesting because, despite some recent falls in oil prices due to potential peace deals, prices are still significantly higher than before the war. It's a reminder that these global events can have a lasting impact on the market. I find it fascinating how interconnected everything is – from geopolitics to our everyday fuel costs.

US stock futures steady post record Wall St close; Iran strikes, PCE data in focus is also worth a quick mention. The point is not to force it into a giant theory; it is simply part of today's news mix.

Lennar (LEN) Exceeds Market Returns: Some Facts to Consider is also worth a quick mention. The stock exceeded the S&P 500, which registered a gain of 0.02% for the day. Elsewhere, the Dow ga... The point is not to force it into a giant theory; it is simply part of today's news mix.

My quick read is that today's set of stories is useful because it gives us texture, not a sermon. Each item has its own little job: one tells us what changed, another tells us what people are worried about, and another gives the day its mood.
So that is the brief version: a few headlines, a little context, and just enough connective tissue to make the day easier to follow without turning the whole thing into homework.
